nodes.title: カタカナ completion sprint
nodes.summary: Week 1 sprint, 15 min/day: Tofugu mnemonics chart, then drill the confusables シツソン and クタケ until they stop being a coin flip. Pass bar: read 30 random katakana loanwords in under 90 seconds — they are mostly English, so this is free vocabulary.

nodes.title: WaniKani — set the limits
nodes.summary: Before a single new lesson, install the community's burnout rules: ≤15 lessons/day, Apprentice ≤100, reviews ≤150/day. If Apprentice climbs past 100, zero new lessons until it drops. This one rule is the #1 quit-preventer in the whole plan.

nodes.title: J-SRS — Anki, kept separate
nodes.summary: Genki vocabulary and grammar clozes in their own Anki profile, never mixed with the German deck. Reviews first always, new cards only after reviews clear, every answer said aloud. New/day: 5 Leisure, 8 Standard, 12 Accelerated.

nodes.title: Genki 1 — XはYです
nodes.summary: The copula sentence, question particle か, and noun の noun. は marks the topic, not the subject — nail that distinction now or every particle explanation for the next year lands slightly wrong.

nodes.title: WaniKani L1–4
nodes.summary: Radicals and the first kanji at a target ~10–12 days per level. Two short sessions a day beat one long one; your level pace is set by the review load, never by how fast the level-up button appears.

nodes.title: Nihongo con Teppei 入門
nodes.summary: 10 minutes a day of comprehensible input from week one. You will not understand much at first, and that is the design — acquisition comes from messages you mostly understand (Krashen's i+1), which needs volume, not perfection.

nodes.title: 数字・時間・電話番号
nodes.summary: Numbers, telling time, phone numbers and the first counters. Boring, extremely high-frequency, and the difference between surviving a shop or a station and not — drill it aloud, not by re-reading the table.

nodes.title: これ/それ/あれ・ここ/そこ
nodes.summary: The こそあど demonstrative grid, plus この+noun and 誰の. Output milestone: walk round your flat and name ten objects out loud with no English in between.

nodes.title: J-OUT — 3-sentence journal
nodes.summary: One output session per week from week 4: three handwritten sentences using this week's grammar point. Kana always, kanji as soon as WaniKani has taught it. Fluency first, accuracy after — finish the thought, then fix it, and every correction becomes an Anki card.

nodes.title: も・じゃないです・ね/よ
nodes.summary: Noun negation じゃないです, the 'too' particle も, and the ね/よ sentence-enders that carry most of the social temperature of spoken Japanese. Small pieces, constant payoff.

nodes.title: WaniKani L5–7
nodes.summary: Kanji count climbs and so does the review load. Watch the Apprentice counter, not the level number: over 100 means stop lessons, clear the backlog, then resume. Reviews ≤150/day is a hard ceiling, not a suggestion.

nodes.title: Tadoku graded readers L0–1
nodes.summary: [N] until Genki ch.6, then [P]. Free level-0 readers: no dictionary on the first pass, at most five lookups on the second, read aloud on the third. Genuinely optional this early — never let it crowd out Genki or WaniKani.

nodes.title: Genki 3 — verb ます/ません
nodes.summary: Your first verbs, in the polite non-past. Learn every verb together with its group (う-verb / る-verb / irregular) from day one — that grouping is what て-form, potential, passive and causative all key off later.

nodes.title: Comprehensible Japanese
nodes.summary: Visual beginner input: gestures and whiteboard drawings carry the meaning, so comprehension stays comfortably ahead of your grammar. Listen once blind, once with support, steal three phrases into Anki.

nodes.title: を・で・に・へ
nodes.summary: The core particle set: を object, で place-of-action and means, に destination and time, へ direction. Particles ARE the grammar in Japanese — drill them inside whole sentences, never as a table to memorise.

nodes.title: あります/います・位置
nodes.summary: Existence split by animacy, plus the location words 上/下/前/後ろ/中/となり. Output task: describe your room in eight sentences — the first point where Japanese starts describing your actual life.

nodes.title: WaniKani L8–10
nodes.summary: Checkpoint: L8–10 by Dec 2026 keeps you on the Standard track, roughly 600 items known by month 4. If you are behind, slow the incoming lessons — never skip reviews to catch up.

nodes.title: 過去形 — でした・ました
nodes.summary: Past tense of です and of verbs, plus 〜時間 duration expressions. From here 'what I did yesterday' becomes a repeatable journal prompt, and past tense is survival skill number one.

nodes.title: い-adjectives
nodes.summary: い-adjectives conjugate like verbs — present, past and negative all happen on the adjective itself, with です adding politeness only. Producing 高くなかった cold is the pass test.

nodes.title: Shadowing Genki dialogues
nodes.summary: Take 30–60 s of audio you already understand and repeat aloud about a beat behind, three passes. Shadowing measurably improves listening and phoneme perception (Hamada 2016) and gets pitch accent into your ear before bad habits set.

nodes.title: な-adjectives・好き/嫌い
nodes.summary: な-adjectives behave like nouns and let です carry the tense — the exact opposite of い-adjectives, which is why they are taught together and confused forever. 好き/嫌い arrive here and take が, not を.

nodes.title: 〜ませんか・〜ましょう
nodes.summary: Inviting with a negative question and suggesting with ましょう. Tiny grammar, big social return — these are your first structures that do something to another person rather than describe the world.

nodes.title: て-form — the workhorse
nodes.summary: The highest-leverage form in Genki I: drill the conjugation groups hard, with the て-form song if it helps, until they are automatic rather than computed. Requests, permission, prohibition, progressive, sequencing and half of Genki II all hang off this one form.

nodes.title: 〜てください
nodes.summary: The everyday request. Trivial once て-form is automatic, which is precisely the point of drilling て-form first — and it is the bottom rung of the politeness ladder you climb in Genki 16.

nodes.title: 〜てもいい・〜てはいけません
nodes.summary: Permission and prohibition. Asking 〜てもいいですか is one of the highest-frequency things a learner actually needs to say in Japan, so drill it as a spoken chunk, not as a written pattern.

nodes.title: て-chaining・から
nodes.summary: Link three actions in one sentence and give reasons with から. This is the week your sentences stop being one clause long — chain three actions in a single breath as the output milestone.

nodes.title: 〜ている
nodes.summary: Progressive AND resultant state: 結婚しています means 'is married', not 'is getting married'. English maps badly here, so learn the resultant-state verbs (結婚する, 住む, 知る, 持つ) as an explicit list.

nodes.title: 人の描写・Vstem+に行く
nodes.summary: 背が高い, 髪が長い: Japanese describes people with が-phrases, not with adjectives stacked before the noun. Plus verb-stem + に行く for 'go somewhere to do something'.

nodes.title: Short forms (辞書形・ない形)
nodes.summary: Dictionary and ない forms — the gateway to casual speech, quotation, opinions, relative clauses, conditionals and nearly every Genki II structure. Second only to て-form in leverage; treat a weak short form as an emergency.

nodes.title: Casual speech・のが好き
nodes.summary: Drop です/ます, add 何か/何も and のが好き for 'like doing'. Essential for understanding anything real — but keep polite forms as your default output register until someone downshifts first.

nodes.title: 〜と思います・〜と言っていました
nodes.summary: Opinions and reported speech both take short forms before と, even when the sentence itself is polite. Write five 〜と思います per journal page until the short form inside a polite sentence stops feeling wrong.

nodes.title: Past short forms・まだ〜ていません
nodes.summary: Casual past, quoted past thoughts, and まだ〜ていません for 'haven't done it yet' — never まだしません. Pass test: a mini-diary written entirely in short forms.

nodes.title: 比較 — より・一番
nodes.summary: AよりBのほうが, 一番, AとBとどちら, plus どこかに/どこにも. Comparison is where you shift from stating facts to stating preferences — 60 seconds aloud comparing your Germany and Japan plans.

nodes.title: 〜たい・〜つもり・adj+なる
nodes.summary: Wanting to do something conjugates like an い-adjective and its object may take が. Bundled with 〜つもりだ (intention) and adjective+なる (becoming) — the 'what I want to do in Japan' journal page.

nodes.title: 〜たり〜たり・〜ことがある
nodes.summary: Listing representative actions and stating experience — both are built on the past short form, which is exactly why they sit after it and not before. Add や for non-exhaustive noun lists.

nodes.title: 〜んです
nodes.summary: The explanatory の/ん — what makes a question sound curious instead of like an interrogation. Learners overuse it in writing and underuse it in speech; get the feel from listening, not from the rule.

nodes.title: 〜ほうがいい・〜ので・〜なきゃ
nodes.summary: Advice with 〜ほうがいいです (past short form — the classic slip), soft reasons with ので, obligation with 〜なきゃいけません, plus 〜すぎる and でしょう. The last new machinery in Genki I.

nodes.title: Genki I 完了 — consolidation
nodes.summary: No new material: re-drill every miss from ch.1–12 interleaved, one big cumulative test, and a 2-minute recorded self-intro to set beside your week-1 recording. Score ≥80% and advance — the 80% rule, not perfection.

nodes.title: N5 mock
nodes.summary: Sit the official JLPT N5 sample papers under exam timing. ≥70% is the green light to book JFT-Basic. Cumulative to this point ≈130 h from today — the N5-equivalent benchmark in Part 6.

nodes.title: WaniKani L11–13
nodes.summary: Checkpoint: L13–14 by Apr 2027. This is the level band where rushers hit 300–500 reviews/day and quit — hold the ≤15 lessons/day line hardest exactly when a level feels easy.

nodes.title: NHK News Web Easy
nodes.summary: Real news with furigana, from N5+ onward. One article a session: no dictionary on the first pass, ≤5 lookups on the second, read aloud on the third. Reading a full article with lookups is the Phase 1 exit test.

nodes.title: JFT-Basic attempt
nodes.summary: Book through Prometric Indonesia in the Dec 2026–Feb 2027 window, once the N5 mock clears 70%. Passing banks the SSW language requirement years early; failing is a free diagnostic on an exam that runs monthly. Either way you win.

nodes.title: Potential 〜られる/〜える
nodes.summary: 話せる, 食べられる — 'can do', and note the object often flips from を to が. Genki II opens here, and the verb groups you drilled for て-form pay their second dividend.

nodes.title: J-OUT — conversation practice
nodes.summary: Weekly spoken output: self-talk built on today's grammar point, or a conversation pitched at your exact Genki chapter. Producing forces syntactic processing that listening lets you skip (Swain) — ugly output now beats perfect output never.

nodes.title: 〜し・なら
nodes.summary: Stacking reasons with 〜し and topic-shifting with なら, plus frequency-per-period expressions. Both attach to short forms, so they land almost for free now.

nodes.title: 〜そうです(様態)・〜てみる
nodes.summary: 'Looks like', formed from the adjective or verb stem, plus 〜てみる 'try doing'. Keep it strictly apart from the hearsay 〜そうです in ch.17 — same word, different attachment, a classic N4 trap.

nodes.title: Teppei 通常版 — N4 listening
nodes.summary: Graduate from the beginner feed to the regular one. Speed, not vocabulary, is what breaks N4 listening — daily minutes at real tempo matter far more than picking harder material.

nodes.title: ほしい・〜かもしれません
nodes.summary: Wanting a thing (が, not を) versus wanting to do a thing (〜たい), plus hedging with かもしれません and quantity+も/しか. Japanese hedges constantly; flat assertions sound blunt.

nodes.title: あげる/くれる/もらう
nodes.summary: The perspective system — genuinely new machinery with no English equivalent. くれる only works when the action moves toward you or your in-group; get the direction wrong and the sentence is not merely odd, it is rude. Drill with arrows on paper.

nodes.title: Volitional 〜よう・〜ておく
nodes.summary: 行こう — 'let's' and 'I think I'll', the casual counterpart of ましょう and the base for 〜ようと思っています. Bundled with 〜ておく, doing something in advance.

nodes.title: Noun-modifying clauses
nodes.summary: Japanese puts the whole clause BEFORE the noun — 私が昨日買った本. There is no 'which/that', and the verb inside goes short form. Reorder-your-brain territory: drill by taking English relative clauses and building them backwards.

nodes.title: Kanji tracing practice
nodes.summary: [N] Trace the kanji WaniKani is teaching you, stroke order included. Motor encoding measurably helps character recognition (Longcamp 2005), five minutes is enough, and it is the cheapest way to break a leech.

resources.label: Kanji grid paper + WaniKani level list

nodes.title: 〜てあげる/くれる/もらう
nodes.summary: The giving system applied to actions rather than objects. Same direction rules, now with a whole verb attached — this is the chapter where Japanese politeness stops looking arbitrary and starts looking like geometry.

nodes.title: 〜ていただけませんか・〜といい
nodes.summary: The request ladder from 〜てください up through 〜てくれない, 〜てもらえますか, 〜ていただけませんか, plus 〜といいですね and 〜てすみませんでした. Register is a real skill in Japanese, not a garnish.

nodes.title: 〜時 (とき) clauses
nodes.summary: 'When...' clauses take short forms, and the tense before 時 is relative to the main clause, not to now. That relative-tense rule is what trips everyone — 日本に行くとき vs 行ったとき mean different halves of the trip.

nodes.title: 〜たら
nodes.summary: The most general conditional, built straight off the past short form. Genki gives you four conditionals in total; たら is the one to over-use while the other three settle into place.

nodes.title: 〜そうです(伝聞)・〜みたい
nodes.summary: Hearsay そうです attaches to short forms; the 'looks like' one from ch.13 attaches to stems. Same word, different attachment, guaranteed exam question. Plus casual quoting 〜って and 〜みたいです.

nodes.title: WaniKani L14–16
nodes.summary: Checkpoint: L16+ by Jun 2027 covers the N4 kanji roughly twice over, with margin. From here WaniKani is exam insurance rather than the bottleneck — reading speed is.

nodes.title: 〜前に・〜てから・〜なくてもいい
nodes.summary: Ordering events precisely: 〜前に takes the dictionary form regardless of when it happened, 〜てから gives 'after doing'. Plus 〜なくてもいいです, 'you don't have to'.

nodes.title: 自動詞・他動詞 pairs
nodes.summary: 開ける/開く, つける/つく, 出す/出る — paired verbs where the particle changes with the member you pick. Learn them in pairs, out loud, in sentences; done badly they haunt every N4 reading passage.

nodes.title: Anime with Japanese subtitles
nodes.summary: Rewatch shows whose plot you already know, Japanese subtitles on. Known story means comprehensible input — this is fluency-strand work, done fast and often, not a dictionary exercise.

nodes.title: 〜てしまう・〜と・〜ながら
nodes.summary: Completion and regret with 〜てしまう (and its spoken 〜ちゃう), the automatic-consequence conditional 〜と, simultaneous actions with 〜ながら, and 〜ばよかった for hindsight. A dense chapter — split it over two weeks.

nodes.title: 尊敬語 — honorific
nodes.summary: いらっしゃる, 召し上がる, なさる and the お〜になる pattern: you raise the other person. Half a dozen irregular verbs carry most of the real-world load — memorise those cold, derive the rest.

nodes.title: 〜はずです・〜てくれてありがとう
nodes.summary: 'Supposed to / ought to be' — expectation grounded in evidence, distinct from かもしれません and でしょう. Plus thanking someone for an action rather than a thing.

nodes.title: 謙譲語 — humble
nodes.summary: Now you lower yourself: 伺う, 申す, いたす, お〜する. The mirror of 尊敬語, and the pair together is what finally makes shop and service Japanese parse. Worth extra drill if SSW work is the goal.

nodes.title: Embedded questions・〜やすい
nodes.summary: 〜か知っていますか and 〜かどうか, plus 〜という〜, 〜ないで and 〜やすい/にくい. Embedded questions keep short forms and lose the question intonation — a frequent N4 reading structure.

nodes.title: 受け身 — passive
nodes.summary: Including the 'suffering passive' — 雨に降られた — which has no English equivalent and is an N4 reading favourite. The conjugation is by verb group again; the semantics are the part that needs drilling.

nodes.title: 〜てある・〜間に・〜てほしい
nodes.summary: 〜てある marks a state somebody deliberately left behind — the transitive twin of 〜ている, and the reason the trans/intrans pairs had to come first. Plus 〜間に 'while' and 〜てほしい 'want someone to'.

nodes.title: 使役 — causative させる
nodes.summary: Make or let someone do — Japanese does not distinguish the two, context and particle choice (に vs を) do. Same conjugation machinery as the passive, which is why it sits directly after it.

nodes.title: 〜ば・〜のに・〜のような
nodes.summary: The fourth conditional, plus 〜のに for 'even though / disappointment' and 〜のような. Lay all four conditionals side by side here — たら, と, ば, なら — because telling them apart is a standing N4 exam question.

nodes.title: 使役受け身
nodes.summary: 'Was made to do' — the last piece of Genki II's verb machinery, and structurally just the two forms you already own, stacked. If passive and causative are solid this is a week; if they are shaky it is a wall.

nodes.title: 〜ことにする・〜ても・〜方
nodes.summary: Deciding (ことにする) versus keeping a habit (ことにしている), concession with 〜ても, 〜まで, and the 〜方 'way of doing' nominaliser. The tidy-up chapter before consolidation.

nodes.title: Genki II 完了 — consolidation
nodes.summary: A full sweep of ch.13–23: interleaved grammar test, every miss into Anki, one long spoken monologue. N4 grammar is now complete on paper — the exam block's job is turning it into speed.

nodes.title: N4 公式問題集 — untimed
nodes.summary: Weeks 1–2 of the exam block: one official practice workbook, untimed, then an error autopsy. Every miss becomes an Anki card carrying the reason, not just the right answer.

nodes.title: N4 grammar gap sweep
nodes.summary: Weeks 3–4: sweep the points Genki underweights, using a drill book [N: 日本語総まとめ N4 or TRY! N4]. You are hunting gaps here, not re-teaching yourself things you already produce correctly.

resources.label: TRY! N4
resources.ref: TRY! 日本語能力試験 N4

nodes.title: N4 timed mocks ×2
nodes.summary: Week 5: two full mocks on the exam clock. Timing is the N4 killer — budget ≤8 minutes per reading passage set and rehearse abandoning a question on schedule rather than finishing it beautifully.

nodes.title: JLPT N4 — 受験
nodes.summary: The July 2027 sitting; register in March, the Indonesian window closes fast. Week 6 is taper: reviews only, listening daily, sleep. Cumulative to here ≈300 h from today — the Part 6 working number for N4.

nodes.title: WaniKani L17–25
nodes.summary: [N] Post-N4 maintenance and the start of the N3 kanji range. The limits never expire — Apprentice ≤100 and reviews ≤150/day are what make a multi-year WaniKani run survivable rather than a slow-motion quit.

nodes.title: N3 route — decide Aug 2027
nodes.summary: [N] Roughly +450–600 h via Tobira or Quartet, WaniKani to ~L30 and heavy native input. Only worth it if your SSW sector rewards it. Decision point is the Aug 2027 check-in, not now — this node sits well beyond the 300 h N4 line.
